title: Chapter 21: Ice and Fury
paragraphs: The first strike of the Reaper wave was like a heavy rock collapsing onto a glass wall. The air exploded with the screech of tearing chitin, the hiss of acid, and a primal roar.

Kirah took the brunt of the mass. The giant demon, whose body had been forged in the furnaces of that very same Abyss, crashed into the ranks of his former brethren like a black meteor. His claws, long as elven sabers, ripped through the gray shells of the creatures, splashing pungent, dark-green ichor around. Kirah did not use destruction magic, fearing to hit the refugees standing behind—he tore the monsters apart with bare, blood-smeared paws, flinging aside two Reapers trying to flank him.

To the demon's right, Dzhanavar scurried as a gray, blurred spot. The werewolf had forgotten his lazy habits and quotes. He moved with the frightening, deadly grace of an enraged beast. His double-bladed axe hissed through the air, each strike accompanied by a wet crunch of severed limbs.

"C'mon, step up, ashen freaks!" Dzhanavar roared, dodging the crescent-shaped blade of a monster and with a wide swing driving his blade straight into its faceted eyes. "A wolf on the hunt knows no fatigue!"

To the left, Rene led a dance of death. The fox glided between the Reapers' mandibles, graceful and elusive as a wisp of her own minty smoke. Her thin, poisoned daggers unerringly found the vulnerable joints on the monsters' necks. She moved not like a warrior on a field of battle, but like a ballerina on a stage where the slightest mistake meant an agonizing death.

And Yashil... the little goblin stood right behind their backs, holding a rusty iron pipe with two shaking hands. He had neither the strength of the demon, nor the speed of the werewolf, nor the agility of the fox. All he could do was finish off those small parasitic creatures that managed to slip past his friends, protecting the refugees huddled in terror.

Each swing of his heavy pipe echoed with burning pain in his weak muscles. Green blood flooded his face; his breath came from his chest with a whistle. He knew they were doomed. There were four of them against thousands.

High above them, on the balcony of the Great Gates, the elven captain froze. He stood, his fingers clutching the marble parapet in a death grip. Tears of impotence rolled down his pale face. His warrior-guards stood frozen beside him, their crossbows lowered, unable to look at this slaughter. They, the High Elves clad in gold plate, had hidden behind an impenetrable magical shield, watching as the "wasteland filth"—a goblin, a demon, a werewolf, and a fox—gave their lives protecting those whom the elves had abandoned to die.
This picture broke their pride more painfully than any weapon. For the first time in centuries, their armor seemed to them not a symbol of greatness, but a cage for cowards.
The slaughter at the gates turned into a meat grinder.

Kirah began to give way. The huge demon was all covered in steaming acid wounds. One of the Reapers managed to drive its bone spike deep into the giant's shoulder. Kirah howled in pain, tearing the creature's head off, but sank to one knee, leaning heavily on the ground. Dzhanavar, whose fur was matted with blood, missed a glancing blow to his ribs, reeling back with a muffled groan. Rene was breathing heavily, her graceful movements had lost their former speed; one of her daggers was broken in half.
The wave of monsters seemed infinite. The creatures stepped over the corpses of their kin, their mandibles clicking in anticipation of imminent massacre.

Yashil stepped forward, shielding the kneeling Kirah. The goblin raised his rusty pipe, preparing to take the final blow. There was no fear in his green eyes. Only absolute, crystal-clear confidence that he had done everything right.
And at that moment, when the first Reaper in a leap raised its sickles over the goblin's head...
The air temperature dropped sharply, unnaturally to a critical level. The ground under the paws of the leaping monster was instantly covered with a crust of thick, transparent ice.

The flying Reaper suddenly froze in the air, pierced by dozens of long, razor-sharp ice spears that erupted straight from the frozen ground. The creature shattered into hundreds of frozen shards with a disgusting crunch, showering down in a gray hail at Yashil's feet.
Dzhanavar blinked in shock, wiping blood from his muzzle. Rene lowered her broken dagger.

A sharp, piercing cold rolled across the battlefield in a wave, freezing the acid blood of the Reapers and binding their movements. Through the mist and ash, from the dark ravines behind the attackers, came a monotonous, frightening march. The clang of old, rusted armor and the thud of bones against stone.

From the impenetrable gloom, a legion of the dead emerged onto the battlefield. Skeletons of gnomes, elves, and half-orks from long-gone eras, whose empty eye sockets glowed with a deathly blue necrotic light. They moved in perfect formation, crashing into the flank of the Abyss army without fear, without pain, and without doubt.

Ahead of this legion of death, stepping on the frozen ground with the grace of a predator, walked a tall man in a flawless black velvet cloak. His pale, sculpted face expressed nothing but centuries-long boredom, but in his eyes the color of clotted blood danced an icy, ruthless flame.

Lord Valtor stopped twenty paces from the exhausted outcasts. With a wave of an elegant, black-leather-clad hand, he sent another cascade of ice spears into the thick of the Reapers, tearing their formation apart.
Rene let out a quiet, hysterical laugh, leaning her back against the jade wall.
"I told you so," the fox whispered, sliding down the smooth stone. "This bloodsucker loves theatrical effects too much to miss the finale."

The vampire paid no attention to the battle that erupted with new force thanks to his undead. He approached the little goblin who was still clutching his rusty pipe. Valtor looked at the green blood staining Yashil's face, at the wounded demon and the heavily breathing werewolf.

"I told you, goblin, that I would come to see how long your naive light would last when the heavens tore apart," Valtor's voice was quiet, velvety, but it carried over the clang of steel and the screech of monsters. "I was sure I would find only your ashes here. I believed that in the face of the absolute end, you would turn into the same animals from which you tried to save this crowd."
Valtor turned his gaze to the refugees—gnomes, hydrols, women, and children who looked at the goblin as if at a deity.

"The High Elves closed the gates. They acted like cowards, justifying it with the cold logic of survival," the vampire continued, and a rare, sincere, almost warm half-smile flickered on his pale lips. "And you, having nothing but a piece of rusty iron, proved to an immortal that there is still something in this world worth saving."
Valtor elegantly, with a slight bow, unsheathed a long, thin blade of blued steel, whose edge emitted a thick frosty mist.
"My legion will hold the flanks, Yashil of the Rotten Hollow," the vampire lord spoke. "But the center remains yours. Show them how those who are not afraid die."
Yashil nodded slowly. The icy light of Valtor's blade was reflected in his green eyes.
A Goblin, a Demon, a Werewolf, a Fox, and an ancient vampire lord.

Five outcasts whose names were never written in the golden folios of the High Elves stood shoulder to shoulder in the epicenter of the decaying reality. The battle for the lives of the rejected at the Great Gates of Agdash flared with new, fierce force, breaking a breach in hopelessness itself.
